metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: AC13 (style check) names no deterministic proving command and is framed as a manual human review ('reviewed section-by-section against the pink-elephant rule stated above'). There is no repo artifact, CI check, or deterministic command that produces evidence on success, making it neither in-session-evaluable by an automated agent nor falsifiable by CI.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the Step 4 heading AC's proving command to assert 'RED oracle' in the heading text: `grep -n '^## Step 4.*RED oracle' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md`
findings.finding: The Step 4 heading AC's proving command `grep -n '^## Step 4' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md` matches ANY '## Step 4' line — it does NOT assert that 'RED oracle' appears in the heading. The prove narrative says 'prints a heading containing RED oracle' but the grep pattern has no such anchor, so the command passes even if the heading reads '## Step 4 – Design a test' (no 'RED oracle').
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'rebar-debug Step 4 is retitled to RED-oracle design. Prove: grep -n ...'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the proving command with: `grep -n '^## Step 4.*RED oracle' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a scoped awk command to the taxonomy AC that constrains the `test-design.md` hit to the Phase 2 section, e.g.: `awk '/Phase 2/,/Phase 3/' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md | grep -q 'test-design.md'`
findings.finding: The llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 AC uses `grep -c 'test-design.md' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md >= 1` but this only proves the string exists somewhere in the file — it does NOT prove the hit is inside the Phase 2 (Repair) bullet. The plan's requirement 'with the hit inside the Phase 2 bullet' has no machine-verifiable proving command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 (Repair) bullet is QUALIFIED. Prove: grep -c ...'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace or augment the grep-c command with: `awk '/Phase 2.*Repair/,/Phase [3-9]/' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md | grep -q 'test-design.md'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the asymmetry between SKILL.md Step 4 ('supersedes' single-eval description) and llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 ('keeps' single-eval definition + adds citation): either (a) reorder the taxonomy's Phase 2 Repair bullet so the citation to test-design.md leads and the single-eval description becomes a backward-compat note, or (b) specify in the plan that the taxonomy's Phase 2 leading clause is also reworded to make test-design.md the primary definition rather than a trailing qualifier.
findings.finding: After the planned edits, the LLM-surface RED-artifact standard will exist at two sites with asymmetric authority: SKILL.md Step 4 will 'supersede' the single-eval description by citing test-design.md, while llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 Repair will *keep* 'RED = an eval/behavioural assertion that fails against the current instruction' as its leading clause and only add a citation. An executor in Phase 2 is directed to read the taxonomy (Stage 2: 'work from llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md'), where the locally-visible single-eval definition will outcompete the deferred citation to test-design.md — a textbook instruction-locality gap (mode #16 in the taxonomy's own failure taxonomy).
findings.location: Skill integration section — rebar-debug changes; 'llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md' Phase 2 (Repair) bullet; SKILL.md Step 4 LLM-surface paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md edit spec, change 'keeps its RED-artifact definition and adds a citation' to: replaces the standalone RED definition with a forward reference — 'RED = the artifact specified in test-design.md (quantified eval: multiple samples, predeclared threshold, negative control; see that file for the carve-out for deterministic assertions)' — so the taxonomy's Phase 2 Repair bullet no longer leads with the single-eval definition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the LLM-evals carve-out in the shared file spec to comply with the plan's own prompt-style rule: lead with the positive requirement (multiple samples, predeclared threshold, negative control), state the carve-out as a single terse conditional ('exception: deterministic-assertion evals at temperature 0 may use a single sample; state the determinism basis'), and remove the elaboration of the excluded rationale ('cost or inconvenience is never grounds').
findings.finding: The plan specifies that test-design.md's LLM-evals section includes a 'narrow carve-out' that 'a single-sample RED/GREEN pair is acceptable ONLY when the assertion is deterministic under the pinned settings … and the report states that determinism; cost or inconvenience is never grounds for skipping quantification.' This named, elaborated carve-out for the prohibited single-sample path — co-located with the prohibition — risks a pink-elephant effect: the model is told 'never do X, except when [detailed conditions for X]', which raises the salience of path X and creates a rationalization pathway ('cost or inconvenience is never grounds' is itself a narration of a common rationalization). The plan's own prompt-style rule states: 'never elaborate negative examples that prime the model toward the prohibited behavior.'
findings.location: What section — LLM-surface evals bullet of shared file specification; Prompt style rule

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the Step 4 edit spec: explicitly state whether the existing single-eval clause is removed (replaced by the test-design.md reference) or retained as a backward-compat note subordinated to the new standard — so the implementer has an unambiguous instruction.
findings.finding: The plan's SKILL.md Step 4 edit directive says the existing LLM-surface paragraph is 'updated to cite the shared file's quantified-eval requirements as the governing RED-artifact standard, superseding its current single-eval description' — but it does not specify whether the old single-eval clause ('the RED artifact is an eval or behavioural assertion that fails against the current instruction') is physically removed or retained alongside the new citation. If retained, Step 4 will contain two competing definitions of the LLM-surface RED standard in the same paragraph, with the simpler existing clause appearing first (more locally salient).
findings.location: Skill integration — rebar-debug/SKILL.md Step 4 LLM-surface paragraph edit spec
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'updated to cite the shared file's quantified-eval requirements as the governing RED-artifact standard, superseding its current single-eval description' to 'replaced: the single-eval clause is removed and substituted with a forward reference to test-design.md as the governing standard for LLM-surface RED artifacts.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the copies-not-symlinks rationale (Windows checkout materialization, zip-archive dangling) in a durable repo artifact — either the README itself (extend the convention section) or a brief CLAUDE.md / ADR entry — so the decision survives ticket archival.
findings.finding: The novel architectural decision — copies-not-symlinks and why — is documented only in the ticket description (ephemeral), not in a durable repo artifact (README, CLAUDE.md, or ADR). A future contributor reading only the repo will see the operational how (sync one-liner) but not the architectural why (Windows symlink materialization, zip-archive dangling), and may introduce symlinks or a different sharing mechanism without realizing the constraint.
findings.location: What section — 'Symlinks were considered and rejected' reasoning; README.md documentation bullet in Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the README convention section with a 'Why copies, not symlinks' paragraph summarizing: symlinks on Windows without Developer Mode appear as one-line text files in checkouts; GitHub zip downloads materialize symlinks as text stubs; a plain copy of a skill directory leaves a symlink dangling. Then update the AC's README grep to assert the word 'symlink' or 'Windows' appears in the README, making the rationale machine-verifiable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `test_skill_shared_sync.py` follows the **pytest byte-comparison** pattern (like `test_prompt_front_matter.py`'s byte-for-byte check) rather than the generate-and-diff script pattern, and explicitly state whether the test needs to be registered in `test_ci_workflow_parity.py`'s `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` to gate pre-merge Verified.
findings.finding: The plan characterizes the new `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py` as 'mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)', but probing the repo shows the actual drift-gate convention for those three cases is generate-and-diff CI workflow steps (separate scripts: `scripts/gen_env_registry.py`, `python -m rebar.schemas.gen_types`, `regenerate-index`) that regenerate an artifact then diff it. The new test is a pytest byte-comparison with no regeneration step — a different pattern. The test_ci_workflow_parity.py registry hardcodes those three drift gates as signatures the new test does NOT share.
findings.location: Plan body — 'mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)' with 'following the repo's pytest byte-comparison pattern (cf. test_prompt_front_matter.py)' and add a note that if the drift gate must also run pre-merge (Gerrit Verified), it needs a signature entry in test_ci_workflow_parity.py.

findings.finding: AC7's proving command for llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md is ambiguous: the second grep (`grep -n 'an eval/behavioural assertion that fails'`) produces zero results if the sentence is *rephrased* and nonzero results if it is *qualified*, but the plan says both outcomes count as passing ('qualified or rephrased'). A zero-result grep conventionally signals failure in a shell pipeline, yet 'rephrased' should also pass — so the command either gives a false-negative when the sentence is correctly removed/reworded, or requires an executor to interpret zero results as a success signal, which is non-obvious and error-prone.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the second grep with two commands: (1) a positive assertion that `test-design.md` appears in the Phase 2 section (already in the first grep, scoped correctly), and (2) a negative assertion `! grep -q 'an eval/behavioural assertion that fails' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` so a passing result unambiguously means the old single-sample language was removed or reworded — zero results = pass for the negative assertion, which is conventional.

findings.finding: The plan body declares a key constraint — 'runs under `make test`, an existing gating CI command, mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention' — but AC bullet 14 (the only CI-gate verification) checks only `make lint` and `.venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py -q`, never `make test`. An implementer who drops the new test file in a location not picked up by `make test` satisfies every AC while violating the stated gating constraint.
findings.location: Plan body ('it runs under `make test`, an existing gating CI command') vs AC bullet 14 (`make lint` and `.venv/bin/pytest ... -q`)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC bullet 14 to: "`make lint`, `make test` (confirm `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py` is collected), and `.venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py -q` all pass."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a deliverable entry in the What section (e.g., under 'Skill integration' or as a separate 'Style verification' step) describing the ticket comment that lists all six edited files with per-file phrasing affirmations, so the 'Style check recorded as an artifact' AC maps to explicitly assigned work.
findings.finding: The 'Style check recorded as an artifact' acceptance criterion — requiring a ticket comment listing every added/edited prompt file with per-file phrasing affirmations — has no corresponding described deliverable in the What section. The What section describes the style constraint ('Prompt style applies to every prompt change...') but never describes the work of producing a verification comment. This is an orphan acceptance criterion: it measures a deliverable the plan body never assigns to the implementer.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Style check recorded as an artifact' bullet; What section — 'Prompt style' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add a line such as: 'Style verification artifact: after all edits are complete, post a comment on this ticket listing each of the six modified files (shared/test-design.md, rebar-debug/SKILL.md, rebar-implement/SKILL.md, rebar-brainstorm/SKILL.md, llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md, README.md) with a one-line affirmation that its added/changed prompt text leads with positive guidance, keeps prohibitions short and direct, and contains no elaborated negative examples.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to `examples/claude-skills/README.md` an explicit note that adding a new consumer requires updating **both** the sync one-liner and the consumer list in `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py`, and add a third `grep` to the README AC (e.g. `grep -q 'test_skill_shared_sync'`) to verify this is present.
findings.finding: The README documentation (and its AC verification) covers only the sync one-liner and the 'byte-identical' concept, but does not require documenting the TWO-PLACE update rule: adding a new consumer skill requires updating BOTH the README one-liner AND the hardcoded consumer list in `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py`. A developer who adds a new consumer skill, copies the file, and updates the one-liner will leave the new copy unprotected by the drift test — the test still passes (it only checks the hardcoded three), silently dropping drift protection for the new consumer.
findings.location: AC: `examples/claude-skills/README.md` documentation bullet; plan section 'Reference mechanism' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: In the README section documenting the convention, add a sentence such as: 'When adding a new consumer skill, update both this one-liner and the consumer list in `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py`.' Then extend the AC to: `grep -q 'byte-identical' examples/claude-skills/README.md && grep -q 'cp examples/claude-skills/shared/test-design.md' examples/claude-skills/README.md && grep -q 'test_skill_shared_sync' examples/claude-skills/README.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief RETIRE note to the README convention and/or the drift-test module docstring specifying that when a consumer skill is removed or renamed, its per-skill copy should be deleted and its entry removed from the drift test's hardcoded consumer list.
findings.finding: The plan specifies what to do when a consumer skill is ADDED (update the drift test + the sync one-liner), but has no specified RETIRE path for when a consumer skill is REMOVED or RENAMED. The orphaned per-skill copy would persist silently, and the hardcoded three-skill drift test would need manual updating with no documented guidance. This is a lifecycle gap in the RETIRE dimension of the shared-state lifecycle.
findings.location: Reference mechanism section ('adding a consumer means adding it to the test and the one-liner together') and AC for the drift test (`tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py`)
findings.suggested_fix: In the README's shared-convention section, add one sentence: 'When a consumer skill is removed or renamed, delete its test-design.md copy and remove it from the consumer list in tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py.' Optionally, add a comment in the drift test's consumer list constant pointing to the README.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
